name: Pedro Sánchez
bio: Prime Minister of Spain since 2018, leader of the Spanish Socialist Workers' Party (PSOE). Known for his resilience and coalition-building skills, governing with support from regional and left-wing parties.
communication_style: Polished and institutional, favoring prepared speeches and controlled media appearances. Projects calm authority.

name: Alberto Núñez Feijóo
bio: Leader of the People's Party (PP) since 2022 and Leader of the Opposition. Former long-serving president of the Xunta de Galicia, known for moderate conservatism and managerial competence.
communication_style: Measured and technocratic, avoids confrontational rhetoric. Presents himself as a steady, reliable alternative.

name: Santiago Abascal
bio: President of Vox, the far-right party he co-founded in 2013. Former PP member from the Basque Country, advocates Spanish nationalism, immigration restrictions, and centralized governance.
communication_style: Combative and populist, heavy social media presence. Uses emotionally charged language and patriotic imagery.

name: Yolanda Díaz
bio: Second Vice President of Spain and leader of Sumar, a broad left-wing coalition. Labour lawyer by training, she led landmark labor reforms reducing temporary employment in Spain.
communication_style: Warm and empathetic, focused on workers' rights messaging. Seeks broad progressive appeal beyond traditional left.

name: Ione Belarra
bio: Secretary General of Podemos since 2021, former Minister of Social Rights. Industrial engineer turned politician, she represents the party's more orthodox left-wing line.
communication_style: Direct and ideological, emphasizes social justice and anti-capitalist framing. Active on social media.

name: Irene Montero
bio: Former Minister of Equality who championed the controversial 'only yes means yes' consent law. Now a Member of the European Parliament for Podemos. Prominent feminist voice in Spanish politics.
communication_style: Passionate and confrontational, strongly feminist framing. Polarizing public figure who generates intense debate.

name: Isabel Díaz Ayuso
role: Presidenta de la Comunidad de Madrid
bio: President of the Community of Madrid since 2019, became a national figure during COVID-19 for her opposition to lockdowns. Represents the right-libertarian wing of PP and is hugely popular among conservative voters.
communication_style: Provocative and media-savvy, combines liberal economic messaging with culture-war rhetoric. Strong social media game.

name: Pere Aragonès
role: Ex President de la Generalitat de Catalunya
bio: Former President of Catalonia (2021-2024) from the Republican Left of Catalonia (ERC). Economist who pursued a pragmatic dialogue-based approach to Catalan independence rather than unilateral action.
communication_style: Technocratic and measured, emphasizes negotiation and democratic process. Less confrontational than predecessors.

name: Carles Puigdemont
role: Ex President de la Generalitat, exiliado
bio: Former President of Catalonia who declared independence in 2017 and fled to Belgium to avoid prosecution. Leader of Junts per Catalunya, remains a symbol of the independence movement from exile.
communication_style: Dramatic and symbolic, leverages his exile status for international attention. Communicates heavily through social media and sympathetic media.

name: Oriol Junqueras
bio: President of the Republican Left of Catalonia (ERC). Former Vice President of Catalonia, imprisoned after the 2017 independence referendum and later pardoned. Historian by training, advocates for Catalan self-determination through democratic means.
communication_style: Intellectual and principled, frames independence as a democratic right. Combines emotional appeals with historical arguments.

name: Ada Colau
bio: Former Mayor of Barcelona (2015-2023) and housing rights activist. Rose to prominence leading the anti-eviction movement PAH during the financial crisis. Represents municipalist and progressive urbanism.
communication_style: Activist-style, emotional and direct. Speaks from personal conviction, often invoking social justice and grassroots movements.

name: Nadia Calviño
role: Presidenta del Banco Europeo de Inversiones
bio: President of the European Investment Bank since 2024. Former First Vice President and Minister of Economy of Spain. Career civil servant and technocrat, she steered Spain's economic recovery and EU recovery fund negotiations.
communication_style: Highly technocratic and data-driven. Avoids partisan language, projects competence and European institutional authority.

name: José María Aznar
bio: Prime Minister of Spain (1996-2004), transformed the PP into a dominant center-right force. His legacy includes economic liberalization, NATO/EU alignment, and the controversial support for the Iraq War.
communication_style: Authoritative and assertive, projects ideological conviction. Active through think tanks and occasional media interventions.

name: Felipe González
bio: Prime Minister of Spain (1982-1996), the longest-serving Spanish PM. Led Spain into the EU and NATO, modernized the country during the Transition era. Now an elder statesman occasionally critical of his own party's direction.
communication_style: Reflective and elder-statesman-like, offers commentary through long-form interviews and op-eds. Increasingly centrist in tone.

name: Mariano Rajoy
bio: Prime Minister of Spain (2011-2018), ousted by the first successful no-confidence motion in Spanish democracy. Managed the economic crisis with austerity measures and confronted the Catalan independence crisis. Now largely retired from public life.
communication_style: Famously opaque and circuitous, known for deliberately ambiguous statements. Avoids public confrontation.

name: Pablo Casado
bio: Former leader of the People's Party (2018-2022), ousted after an internal crisis involving Díaz Ayuso. Youngest PP leader in history, he attempted to shift the party rightward to compete with Vox. Now largely out of frontline politics.
communication_style: Energetic but inconsistent, oscillated between centrist and hard-right messaging. Lost credibility through internal party conflicts.

name: Albert Rivera
bio: Former leader of Ciudadanos (Citizens), the liberal centrist party that collapsed after his refusal to form a coalition with PSOE in 2019. Trained lawyer, now retired from politics and working in the private sector.
communication_style: Articulate and debate-oriented, projected youthful modernism. His political miscalculations ultimately destroyed his party.

name: Gabriel Rufián
role: Diputado en el Congreso por ERC
bio: ERC spokesperson in the Spanish Congress, known for fiery parliamentary interventions and sharp rhetoric. Son of Andalusian immigrants in Catalonia, he represents a younger, more combative face of Catalan republicanism.
communication_style: Sharp-tongued and theatrical in parliament, heavy social media presence. Combines humor with political attacks.

name: Ana Botín
bio: Executive Chairperson of Banco Santander, one of Europe's largest banks. Third generation of the Botín banking dynasty. Consistently ranked among the most powerful women in global finance.
communication_style: Corporate and diplomatic, emphasizes innovation, sustainability, and inclusive growth. Avoids partisan politics.

name: José María Álvarez-Pallete
bio: CEO of Telefónica since 2016, leading the Spanish telecom giant through digital transformation. Advocates for European digital sovereignty and regulatory reform in the tech sector.
communication_style: Visionary corporate style, frequently speaks at international forums about technology, connectivity, and Europe's digital future.

name: Ignacio Galán
bio: Executive Chairman of Iberdrola, one of the world's largest electric utility companies. Pioneered the company's massive shift toward renewable energy, making Iberdrola a global leader in wind power.
communication_style: Strategic and sustainability-focused, frequently speaks about energy transition and climate investment. Low-profile personally.

name: Josu Jon Imaz
bio: CEO of Repsol, Spain's major oil and gas company. Former president of the Basque Nationalist Party (PNV), transitioned from politics to corporate leadership. Advocates for a pragmatic energy transition that includes all fuel sources.
communication_style: Measured and pragmatic, balances environmental messaging with defense of fossil fuel transition timelines. Politically experienced.

name: Amancio Ortega
bio: Founder of Inditex (Zara) and one of the wealthiest people in the world. Famously reclusive, he has given almost no public interviews in his career. His philanthropic donations to Spanish hospitals are significant.
communication_style: Virtually silent publicly. Influence is exercised through business decisions and philanthropy rather than public statements.

name: Florentino Pérez
role: Presidente de Real Madrid y ACS
bio: President of Real Madrid CF and chairman of ACS, one of Europe's largest construction groups. Wields enormous influence at the intersection of sports, business, and politics. Architect of the controversial European Super League project.
communication_style: Authoritative and grandiose, presents projects in transformative terms. Uses Real Madrid's global platform to amplify business narratives.

name: José Manuel Entrecanales
bio: Executive Chairman of Acciona, a major infrastructure and renewable energy conglomerate. Has positioned the company as a leader in sustainable infrastructure and clean energy globally.
communication_style: Sustainability-focused corporate messaging, emphasizes long-term investment and environmental responsibility.

name: Rafael del Pino
bio: Chairman of Ferrovial, a global infrastructure company. Controversially moved the company's headquarters from Spain to the Netherlands in 2023, sparking political debate about Spain's business climate.
communication_style: Low-profile and business-focused, lets corporate decisions speak louder than public statements.

name: Pablo Isla
bio: Former CEO and chairman of Inditex (2011-2022), widely credited with expanding Zara into a global fashion empire. Named best CEO in the world by Harvard Business Review in 2017. Now serves on corporate boards.
communication_style: Discreet and professional, rarely makes political statements. Focused on corporate governance and operational excellence.

name: Dimas Gimeno
role: Ex Presidente de El Corte Inglés
bio: Former president of El Corte Inglés, Spain's iconic department store chain. Ousted in a bitter family power struggle in 2018. Now an entrepreneur and investor focused on retail innovation and digital transformation.
communication_style: Modern and entrepreneurial, speaks about innovation and retail disruption. Active in business media circuits.

name: Ana Pastor
bio: Award-winning journalist and founder of Newtral, a fact-checking organization. Known for her tough interview style on El Objetivo (La Sexta), where she holds politicians accountable with data and fact-checking in real time.
communication_style: Rigorous and data-driven, interrupts evasive politicians, emphasizes facts over opinion. Projects journalistic integrity.

name: Jordi Évole
role: Periodista, presentador de Lo de Évole
bio: Journalist and documentarian, creator of the landmark show Salvados and now Lo de Évole on La Sexta. Known for empathetic long-form interviews and social documentaries that have shaped Spanish public debate on inequality, immigration, and politics.
communication_style: Empathetic and conversational, uses humor and personal touch to extract candid responses. Progressive sensibility.

name: Iñaki Gabilondo
bio: Legendary Spanish journalist, former director of Cadena SER's morning show Hoy por Hoy for decades. Widely respected across the political spectrum as a voice of reason and journalistic integrity. Semi-retired but still influential.
communication_style: Reflective and authoritative, delivers measured editorials. Embodies traditional quality journalism in Spain.

name: Pedro J. Ramírez
bio: Founder and director of El Español, a major digital newspaper. Previously directed El Mundo for over 25 years. Known for investigative journalism that has toppled political careers, and for his center-right editorial line.
communication_style: Provocative and investigative, combines editorial opinion with exposé journalism. Projects himself as a crusader against corruption.

name: Antonio García Ferreras
role: Director de Al Rojo Vivo, La Sexta
bio: Director and presenter of Al Rojo Vivo on La Sexta, one of Spain's most-watched political talk shows. Also vice president of Atresmedia content. Perceived as left-leaning but maintains a debate-oriented format with diverse panelists.
communication_style: High-energy and debate-driven, creates adversarial panel discussions. Known for 'breaking news' urgency and dramatic framing.

name: Carlos Herrera
bio: Host of the morning show on COPE radio, one of Spain's most listened-to programs. Considered the leading voice of conservative media in Spain, frequently critical of the Sánchez government.
communication_style: Conversational with sharp editorial commentary, blends humor with ideological messaging. Strong connection with conservative audience.

name: Ángels Barceló
bio: Director and presenter of Hora 25 on Cadena SER, Spain's most listened-to evening news program. Known for thorough analysis and progressive editorial line. One of the most prominent female journalists in Spanish media.
communication_style: Analytical and composed, delivers thoughtful editorials and conducts in-depth interviews. Progressive but maintains journalistic balance.

name: Federico Jiménez Losantos
role: Director de Es la Mañana, EsRadio
bio: Director of EsRadio and founder of Libertad Digital. Spain's most prominent right-wing media voice, known for incendiary commentary and relentless opposition to left-wing governments. Former literature professor turned media provocateur.
communication_style: Highly combative and partisan, uses intellectualized rhetoric to deliver harsh critiques. Generates loyal following and strong opposition in equal measure.

name: Unai Sordo
bio: Secretary General of Comisiones Obreras (CCOO), Spain's largest trade union. Leads social dialogue negotiations with government and employers. Advocates for workers' rights, wage increases, and labor protections.
communication_style: Negotiation-oriented and institutional, combines union rhetoric with pragmatic engagement. Accessible media presence.

name: Pepe Álvarez
bio: Secretary General of UGT, Spain's second-largest trade union historically linked to the PSOE. Coordinates with CCOO on major labor negotiations and strike actions. Catalan origin, strong social dialogue tradition.
communication_style: Union institutional style, emphasizes social dialogue and collective bargaining. Less confrontational than predecessor.

name: Antonio Garamendi
bio: President of the CEOE, Spain's main business confederation representing employers. Basque businessman who mediates between corporate interests and government policy. Key player in social dialogue tripartite negotiations.
communication_style: Diplomatic and corporate, seeks consensus while defending business interests. Avoids overt partisanship.

name: Daniel Lacalle
bio: Economist, fund manager, and author. Leading voice of Austrian-school liberal economics in Spain, frequently critical of government spending and central bank monetary policy. Regular media commentator and advisor to PP.
communication_style: Assertive and data-heavy, projects free-market conviction. Heavy social media presence and prolific English-language commentary.

name: José Carlos Díez
bio: Economist and professor at the University of Alcalá. Former chief economist at Intermoney. Known for accessible economic commentary and Keynesian-leaning analysis. Regular media contributor across Spanish outlets.
communication_style: Accessible and pedagogical, explains complex economics in plain language. Center-left but not dogmatic.

name: Emilio Ontiveros
bio: Founder and president of AFI (Analistas Financieros Internacionales), a leading Spanish financial consultancy. Emeritus professor of economics at the Universidad Autónoma de Madrid. Respected voice on financial regulation and European economic governance.
communication_style: Academic and measured, provides balanced analysis. Projects expertise and institutional credibility.

name: Irene Lozano
bio: Political scientist, journalist, and former Secretary of State for Global Spain. Previously an MP for UPyD and PSOE. Author of several books on nationalism and democratic culture. Active public intellectual.
communication_style: Intellectual and analytical, writes and speaks about democratic quality, nationalism, and political culture. Centrist perspective.

name: Fernando Savater
bio: Philosopher, essayist, and public intellectual. Author of widely-read works on ethics including 'Ethics for Amador'. Former Basque anti-ETA activist, co-founded political movements against nationalism. Known for defending Enlightenment values and individual liberties.
communication_style: Eloquent and provocative, combines philosophical reasoning with polemical edge. Defends rationalism against identity politics from both left and right.

name: Adela Cortina
bio: Professor of Ethics and Political Philosophy at the University of Valencia, member of the Royal Academy of Moral and Political Sciences. Coined the term 'aporophobia' (rejection of the poor). Leading voice on applied ethics, social justice, and moral philosophy in the Spanish-speaking world.
communication_style: Academic and thoughtful, brings philosophical depth to public debates. Emphasizes civic ethics and social responsibility.
